sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 32-year-old female who presents with symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) characterized by functional abdominal pain, nausea, and diarrhea.
She has a history of flares of abdominal pain, which was diagnosed as IBS and treated with bentyl.
She was previously prescribed reglan for nausea, but was instructed to stop using it and use zofran as needed instead.
The patient has been experiencing symptoms of IBS, including cramping and sharp pains, nausea, and diarrhea, which are exacerbated by spicy foods, fatty foods, and alcohol consumption.
She has noticed that she has been doing better by drinking water instead of sodas.
She has been taking citrucel as prescribed, but has not found it effective.
She has also been experiencing bloating and gas, which is worsened by eating pasta and fried chicken.
The patient has a history of eating out on Fridays and Saturdays, which often involves consuming spicy foods and alcohol.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of IBS, which was diagnosed in 2019 and treated with bentyl.
Review of systems: The patient reports experiencing diarrhea and abdominal pain.
Current medications:
Bentyl 20 mg (up to four times daily for intestinal cramping)
Zofran (as needed for nausea)
Citrucel (twice daily for regularity)
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: Not mentioned
Pulse rate: Not mentioned
Oxygen saturation: Not mentioned
Temperature: Not mentioned
Physical exam:
The patient is alert and cooperative, appears stated age.
Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Heart is regular rate and rhythm.
Abdomen is soft, nontender, no masses or organomegaly, and normal active bowel sounds.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a pleasant 32-year-old female with IBS and functional abdominal pain.
Problem list: IBS; functional abdominal pain; suspected sensitivity to spicy foods, fatty foods, and alcohol; suspected sensitivity to greasy or fatty foods and sugary drinks.
Plan:
I prescribed elavil 25 mg to be taken at bedtime to help manage IBS.
I instructed the patient to use bentyl 20 mg as needed, 30-45 minutes prior to eating out at a restaurant.
I also instructed the patient to reduce greasy and fatty foods and alcohol consumption.
I asked the patient to follow up in 1 month by emailing me regarding any side effects or issues with elavil or if adjustments are needed.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with an email in 1 month to check in on symptoms and any side effects of elavil.
